title: Walks

lean_statement.name: WalkStep
lean_statement.code:
/-- A single edge in a walk in the CDMG `G`, between two adjacent
vertices `v` and `w`, with explicit orientation. The three constructors
mirror the three cases in `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`
def 3.4 item 1: `forward` for `v ⟶[G] w` (`\tuh`), `backward` for
`v ⟵[G] w` (`\hut`, equivalent to `(w, v) ∈ G.E`), and `bidir` for
`v ⟷[G] w` (`\huh`). Each constructor carries the underlying
adjacency proof inline. -/
inductive WalkStep (G : CDMG α) : α → α → Type _ where
  /-- A directed forward step `v → w`, witnessing `(v, w) ∈ G.E`.
  Mirrors LN's `\tuh`. -/
  | forward {v w : α} (h : v ⟶[G] w) : WalkStep G v w
  /-- A directed backward step `v ← w`, witnessing `(w, v) ∈ G.E`.
  Mirrors LN's `\hut`. -/
  | backward {v w : α} (h : v ⟵[G] w) : WalkStep G v w
  /-- A bidirected step `v ↔ w`, witnessing `(v, w) ∈ G.L`. Mirrors
  LN's `\huh`. -/
  | bidir {v w : α} (h : v ⟷[G] w) : WalkStep G v w

lean_statement.name: Walk
lean_statement.code:
/-- A walk in the CDMG `G` from `v` to `w`. Inductively built from a
trivial walk `nil v : Walk G v v` (the LN's trivial single-vertex walk)
by left-consing `WalkStep`s onto a tail walk. See
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` def 3.4 item 1. The same
node may appear multiple times in the support; see `Walk.support` for
the visited-vertex list. -/
inductive Walk (G : CDMG α) : α → α → Type _ where
  /-- The trivial walk on a single vertex `v` (LN's "trivial walk
  consisting of a single node $v_0 \in G$"). -/
  | nil (v : α) : Walk G v v
  /-- Extend a walk by left-consing a leading edge step. -/
  | cons {v w u : α} (s : WalkStep G v w) (p : Walk G w u) : Walk G v u

lean_statement.name: WalkStep.reverse
lean_statement.code:
-- helper required to state Walk.reverse
/-- The orientation-reversed version of a `WalkStep`. Swaps `forward
↔ backward` (a definitional identity on the underlying `G.E`
membership) and conjugates `bidir` by `G.L_symm` from def 3.1. Used by
`Walk.reverse`. -/
def reverse : {v w : α} → WalkStep G v w → WalkStep G w v
  | _, _, .forward h  => .backward h
  | _, _, .backward h => .forward h
  | _, _, .bidir h    => .bidir (G.L_symm h)

lean_statement.name: Walk.length
lean_statement.code:
/-- The *length* of a walk: the number of edges (`WalkStep`s) it
contains. This is the LN's parameter `n` in `v = v_0, …, v_n = w`. -/
def length : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → ℕ
  | _, _, .nil _    => 0
  | _, _, .cons _ p => p.length + 1

lean_statement.name: Walk.support
lean_statement.code:
/-- The list of vertices visited by a walk, in order
(`v_0, v_1, …, v_n` in the LN's notation). It has length
`p.length + 1`; see `Walk.support_length`. -/
def support : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → List α
  | _, _, .nil v    => [v]
  | v, _, .cons _ p => v :: p.support

lean_statement.name: Walk.firstStep?
lean_statement.code:
/-- The first `WalkStep` of a walk, if any: `none` on the trivial
walk, `some ⟨v, w, s⟩` on `cons s _` where `s : WalkStep G v w`.
Packaged as a dependent pair so the step's endpoints remain
inspectable. -/
def firstStep? : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Option (Σ' (v' w' : α), WalkStep G v' w')
  | _, _, .nil _    => none
  | _, _, .cons s _ => some ⟨_, _, s⟩

lean_statement.name: Walk.lastStep?
lean_statement.code:
/-- The last `WalkStep` of a walk, if any. Trivial walk → `none`;
single-step walk → `some` of that step; longer walk → recurse into
the tail. -/
def lastStep? : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Option (Σ' (v' w' : α), WalkStep G v' w')
  | _, _, .nil _              => none
  | _, _, .cons s (.nil _)    => some ⟨_, _, s⟩
  | _, _, .cons _ (.cons s p) => lastStep? (.cons s p)

lean_statement.name: Walk.append
lean_statement.code:
/-- The concatenation of two walks, sharing a common endpoint.
Mathlib's `SimpleGraph.Walk.append` is the structural precedent. -/
def append : {u v w : α} → Walk G u v → Walk G v w → Walk G u w
  | _, _, _, .nil _,    q => q
  | _, _, _, .cons s p, q => .cons s (p.append q)

lean_statement.name: Walk.reverse
lean_statement.code:
/-- The walk in reverse. Defined by `(nil v).reverse = nil v` and
`(cons s p).reverse = p.reverse.append (cons s.reverse (nil v))`. -/
def reverse : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Walk G w v
  | _, _, .nil v    => .nil v
  | v, _, .cons s p => p.reverse.append (.cons s.reverse (.nil v))

lean_statement.name: WalkStep.IsBidir
lean_statement.code:
-- helper required to state IsBidirected / IsCollider
/-- The step is a *bidirected* edge: `s = .bidir _`. Mirrors the
LN's `v \huh w`. -/
def IsBidir : {v w : α} → WalkStep G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .forward _  => False
  | _, _, .backward _ => False
  | _, _, .bidir _    => True

lean_statement.name: WalkStep.HasArrowheadAtTarget
lean_statement.code:
-- helper required to state IsCollider / IntoEnd
/-- The step has an arrowhead at its *target* endpoint: `forward`
or `bidir`. Mirrors the LN's `v \suh w`. -/
def HasArrowheadAtTarget : {v w : α} → WalkStep G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .forward _  => True
  | _, _, .backward _ => False
  | _, _, .bidir _    => True

lean_statement.name: WalkStep.HasArrowheadAtSource
lean_statement.code:
-- helper required to state IsCollider / IntoStart / BifurcationWitness
/-- The step has an arrowhead at its *source* endpoint: `backward`
or `bidir`. Mirrors the LN's `v \hus w`. -/
def HasArrowheadAtSource : {v w : α} → WalkStep G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .forward _  => False
  | _, _, .backward _ => True
  | _, _, .bidir _    => True

lean_statement.name: Walk.IsDirected
lean_statement.code:
/-- A *directed walk*: every step is a `forward` step. Equivalent
to the LN's "all arrowheads point in the direction of $w$ and
there are no arrowheads pointing back" condition. The trivial walk
is directed vacuously. -/
def IsDirected : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _              => True
  | _, _, .cons (.forward _) p => p.IsDirected
  | _, _, .cons (.backward _) _ => False
  | _, _, .cons (.bidir _) _    => False

lean_statement.name: Walk.IsBidirected
lean_statement.code:
/-- A *bidirected walk*: every step is a `bidir` step. The trivial
walk is bidirected vacuously. -/
def IsBidirected : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _              => True
  | _, _, .cons (.bidir _) p   => p.IsBidirected
  | _, _, .cons (.forward _) _ => False
  | _, _, .cons (.backward _) _ => False

lean_statement.name: Walk.IsColliderInner
lean_statement.code:
-- helper required to state IsCollider
/-- Helper for `IsCollider`: a walk whose internal steps are all
bidir and whose final step has an arrowhead at its source endpoint
(LN's `\hus`). Encodes the "$v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus
v_n$" suffix of a collider walk. -/
def IsColliderInner : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _              => True
  | _, _, .cons s (.nil _)    => s.HasArrowheadAtSource
  | _, _, .cons s (.cons s' p) => s.IsBidir ∧ IsColliderInner (.cons s' p)

lean_statement.name: Walk.IsCollider
lean_statement.code:
/-- A *collider walk* in `G` from `v` to `w`: the LN shape
$v_0 \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus v_n$. The trivial walk
is a collider walk vacuously; a single-step walk is a collider
walk iff that step is bidirected (see the design-choice block
above for the $n = 1$ interpretation). -/
def IsCollider : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _              => True
  | _, _, .cons s (.nil _)    => s.IsBidir
  | _, _, .cons s (.cons s' p) =>
      s.HasArrowheadAtTarget ∧ (Walk.cons s' p).IsColliderInner

lean_statement.name: Walk.IsPath
lean_statement.code:
/-- A *path* in `G`: a walk whose visited-vertex sequence has no
repeats. Equivalent to `List.Nodup` on `Walk.support`. -/
def IsPath {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w) : Prop := π.support.Nodup

lean_statement.name: Walk.IntoStart
lean_statement.code:
/-- The walk is *into $v_0$* (LN item 1 prose): the first
`WalkStep` is `backward` or `bidir`, i.e. it has an arrowhead at
the walk's start vertex. The trivial walk is *not* into its start
vertex (it has no first edge). -/
def IntoStart : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _              => False
  | _, _, .cons s _           => s.HasArrowheadAtSource

lean_statement.name: Walk.OutOfStart
lean_statement.code:
/-- The walk is *out of $v_0$* (LN item 1 prose): the first
`WalkStep` is `forward`, i.e. a directed edge originating at
$v_0$. The trivial walk is not out of its start vertex. -/
def OutOfStart : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _              => False
  | _, _, .cons (.forward _) _ => True
  | _, _, .cons (.backward _) _ => False
  | _, _, .cons (.bidir _) _    => False

lean_statement.name: Walk.IntoEnd
lean_statement.code:
/-- The walk is *into $v_n$* (LN item 1 prose): the last
`WalkStep` has an arrowhead at the walk's end vertex
(`HasArrowheadAtTarget`). The trivial walk is not into its end
vertex (no last edge). -/
def IntoEnd : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _              => False
  | _, _, .cons s (.nil _)    => s.HasArrowheadAtTarget
  | _, _, .cons _ (.cons s p) => IntoEnd (.cons s p)

lean_statement.name: Walk.OutOfEnd
lean_statement.code:
/-- The walk is *out of $v_n$* (LN item 1 prose): the last
`WalkStep` is `backward`, i.e. a directed edge with $v_n$ as the
tail. The trivial walk is not out of its end vertex. -/
def OutOfEnd : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _              => False
  | _, _, .cons s (.nil _)    =>
      -- Single-step walk: this step is the last; it must be backward.
      match s with
      | .backward _ => True
      | _           => False
  | _, _, .cons _ (.cons s p) => OutOfEnd (.cons s p)

lean_statement.name: Walk.IsAllBackward
lean_statement.code:
-- helper required to state BifurcationWitness.leftBackward
/-- A walk in which *every step* is a `backward` step (the LN's
`\hut`). The trivial walk is vacuously `IsAllBackward`. The LN's
"the subwalk $v_0 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1}$ is a directed walk from
$v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$" (def 3.4 item 6, left arm of a bifurcation)
is equivalent to this predicate on the left arm. -/
def IsAllBackward : {v w : α} → Walk G v w → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _               => True
  | _, _, .cons (.backward _) p => p.IsAllBackward
  | _, _, .cons (.forward _) _  => False
  | _, _, .cons (.bidir _) _    => False

lean_statement.name: Walk.BifurcationWitness
lean_statement.code:
/-- Witness that a walk `π : Walk G v w` is a bifurcation: an
explicit decomposition into a left arm (all `backward` steps), a
hinge step (with an arrowhead at its source, i.e. `backward` or
`bidir`), and a right arm (all `forward` steps), matching LN
def 3.4 item 6's shape
$v_0 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$. -/
structure BifurcationWitness {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w) : Type _ where
  /-- The LN's $v_{k-1}$: end of the left arm, start of the hinge. -/
  m : α
  /-- The LN's $v_k$: end of the hinge, start of the right arm. -/
  m' : α
  /-- The left arm $v_0 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1}$. Has length
  $k - 1$; may be the trivial walk `nil v` if $k = 1$. -/
  leftArm : Walk G v m
  /-- The hinge step $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$ -- either `backward`
  (LN's `\hut`, giving a source) or `bidir` (LN's `\huh`, no
  source). -/
  hinge : WalkStep G m m'
  /-- The right arm $v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$. Has length
  $n - k$; may be the trivial walk `nil w` if $k = n$. -/
  rightArm : Walk G m' w
  /-- The walk `π` decomposes as `leftArm` followed by the hinge
  step followed by `rightArm`. -/
  decompose : π = leftArm.append (Walk.cons hinge rightArm)
  /-- Every step of the left arm is a `backward` step
  (LN: "$v_0 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1}$"). -/
  leftBackward : leftArm.IsAllBackward
  /-- The hinge step has an arrowhead at its source endpoint,
  i.e. it is `backward` or `bidir` (LN: "$v_{k-1} \hus v_k$"). -/
  hingeIntoSource : hinge.HasArrowheadAtSource
  /-- Every step of the right arm is a `forward` step
  (LN: "$v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$"). -/
  rightDirected : rightArm.IsDirected

lean_statement.name: Walk.IsBifurcation
lean_statement.code:
/-- A walk `π : Walk G v w` is a *bifurcation* between $v$ and $w$
in $G$ (LN def 3.4 item 6) iff $v \ne w$, both endnodes appear
exactly once in `π.support`, and `π` admits a witness
decomposition into a left arm (all `backward`), a hinge step
(`backward` or `bidir`), and a right arm (all `forward`). -/
def IsBifurcation {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w) : Prop :=
  v ≠ w ∧
  v ∉ π.support.tail ∧
  w ∉ π.support.dropLast ∧
  Nonempty (BifurcationWitness π)

lean_statement.name: Walk.bifurcationSource
lean_statement.code:
/-- The *source* of a bifurcation in the LN def 3.4 item 6 sense:
`some bw.m'` if the chosen witness's hinge is `backward` (the
LN's "$v_{k-1} \hut v_k$" directed-hinge case, where the source
is $v_k$), or `none` if it is `bidir` (LN's "$v_{k-1} \huh v_k$"
bidirected-hinge case, where the LN does not assign a source).
The witness is selected by `Classical.choice` from the
`Nonempty (BifurcationWitness π)` clause of `IsBifurcation`; see
the module docstring for the well-definedness caveat. -/
noncomputable def bifurcationSource {v w : α} (π : Walk G v w)
    (hb : π.IsBifurcation) : Option α :=
  let bw := hb.2.2.2.some
  match bw.hinge with
  | .backward _ => some bw.m'
  | _           => none

lean_explanation:
The umbrella context is a CDMG `G : CDMG α` over an arbitrary vertex type `α : Type*` — matching the LN's silent assumption that the graph is over some carrier. The block below covers all six items of LN def 3.4 plus item 1's four "into / out of $v_0$ / $v_n$" prose predicates; per-step helpers (`WalkStep.IsBidir`, `HasArrowheadAtTarget`, `HasArrowheadAtSource`) and small recursion helpers (`IsColliderInner`, `IsAllBackward`, `WalkStep.reverse`) are included because the LN-level definitions reference them.

Walks are encoded as *data*, not propositions. `WalkStep G v w` is a single edge between adjacent vertices, with three constructors `forward` ($v \tuh w$), `backward` ($v \hut w$), `bidir` ($v \huh w$); each constructor carries the underlying adjacency proof inline (`v ⟶[G] w`, `v ⟵[G] w`, `v ⟷[G] w`). `Walk G v w` is the indexed inductive of walks built from `nil v : Walk G v v` (LN's trivial walk) by left-consing `WalkStep`s. The vertex indices on `Walk G v w` are part of the type, which is what lets `cons : WalkStep G v w → Walk G w u → Walk G v u` enforce vertex-matching at compile time.

Derived operations on `Walk` reflect LN-level vocabulary: `length` exposes the LN's $n$ in $v = v_0, \dots, v_n = w$; `support` is the visited-vertex list $v_0, \dots, v_n$; `firstStep?` and `lastStep?` return the boundary `WalkStep`s as `Option (Σ' (v' w' : α), WalkStep G v' w')` — a `PSigma`-packaged dependent pair so callers can inspect the step's endpoints without re-pattern-matching the walk. `append` and `reverse` give concatenation and reversal.

Items 2–5 are `Prop`-valued recursive predicates: `IsDirected`, `IsBidirected`, and `IsCollider` constrain each step's orientation; `IsPath` is `support.Nodup` (Mathlib's no-repeats predicate on lists). The four prose predicates `IntoStart` / `OutOfStart` / `IntoEnd` / `OutOfEnd` pattern-match on the relevant endpoint step's constructor.

Item 6 (bifurcations) is encoded by a `Type _`-valued witness structure `BifurcationWitness π` and a `Prop`-valued predicate `IsBifurcation π`. The witness names the LN's $v_{k-1}, v_k$ (fields `m, m'`), the left arm $v_0 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1}$, hinge step $v_{k-1} \hus v_k$, and right arm $v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$, plus three shape-constraint fields and a `decompose : π = leftArm.append (cons hinge rightArm)` field tying the pieces back to $\pi$. `IsBifurcation` bundles `v ≠ w`, two endpoint-uniqueness clauses phrased without decidable equality, and `Nonempty (BifurcationWitness π)`. `bifurcationSource` picks a witness via `Classical.choice` (hence `noncomputable`) and returns `some bw.m'` for a `backward` hinge, `none` otherwise.
design_choices:
**Walks are data (`Type _`), not propositions.** This is the most consequential decision and is the one that propagates into all downstream rows. Chapter 16 of the lecture notes concatenates, reverses, and inducts on *named* walks ($\pi$); none of that survives if walks are merely existence claims. Existential phrasings used later still work — they read `∃ π : Walk G v w, P π` over the data-valued walk type — but the data layer must exist underneath. A consequence is that each `WalkStep` constructor carries the underlying adjacency proof inline, so the *existence of a walk certifies the existence of its edges*; a `Prop`-valued encoding cannot enforce this.

**Three `WalkStep` constructors, not a single constructor with an orientation enum.** The LN explicitly enumerates `\tuh`, `\hut`, `\huh`, and a single-constructor encoding would force callers to `match` on the enum at every use site — exactly the case-split the LN's prose already does. Three constructors let downstream pattern matches read like the LN's case analysis. They also let `WalkStep.reverse` pattern-match the three cases directly, with `forward ↔ backward` collapsing to `rfl` because both unfold to `(v, w) ∈ G.E`.

**Left-cons inductive `Walk`, not right-cons or a flat list of steps.** Left-cons makes "the first step of a walk" a trivial pattern-match away, which is what item 1's "into / out of $v_0$" prose predicates read off. A right-cons shape would invert that and make `firstStep` recursive. A flat `List (WalkStep G _ _)` would require an external endpoint-matching invariant; the indexed inductive shape bakes it in.

**Vacuous handling of the trivial walk.** Each predicate needs a choice at `nil v` (length $n = 0$). For `IsDirected`, `IsBidirected`, `IsCollider`, `IsPath` the trivial walk is `True` — the LN's "for some $n \ge 0$" admits $n = 0$ and there are no edges to constrain. This is load-bearing downstream: def 3.5's ancestor set $\mathrm{Anc}^G(v)$ needs the trivial directed walk for $v$ to be its own ancestor. For the four prose predicates `IntoStart` / `OutOfStart` / `IntoEnd` / `OutOfEnd` the trivial walk is `False` — each LN sentence references a specific edge $a_0$ or $a_{n-1}$ which the trivial walk does not have.

**Item 4 (collider walk) is interpreted strictly at $n = 1$.** The LN's general formula $v_0 \suh v_1 \huh \cdots \huh v_{n-1} \hus v_n$ instantiated at $n = 1$ collapses to a single edge that is *simultaneously* $\suh$ (arrowhead at $v_1$) and $\hus$ (arrowhead at $v_0$) — i.e. bidirected. We adopt this strict reading: only bidirected single-step walks count as collider walks. The LN's parenthetical "for $n = 1$ this reads: $v \sus w$" is slightly looser (admitting any orientation), but every downstream use of collider walks composes via the strict general formula, so the strict reading is the compositional choice.

**Bifurcations are witnessed by a decomposition structure, not by a numeric hinge index.** The LN's shape $v_0 \hut \cdots \hut v_{k-1} \hus v_k \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$ existentially quantifies over an index $k$. Two natural encodings: (a) a numeric-index predicate threading `takeSteps` and `dropSteps` operations; (b) a witness structure naming the three pieces directly. We picked (b): `BifurcationWitness π` exposes fields for the left arm, hinge step, and right arm, with a `decompose` field tying them back to $\pi$. This sidesteps off-by-one arithmetic on $k$ (the hinge sits at *position* $k - 1$, not $k$), makes the source extractor a pure pattern-match on `bw.hinge`'s constructor, and mirrors the LN's downstream usage pattern: subsequent claims always decompose a bifurcation into its three pieces rather than referring to $k$. The LN's $k$ is still recoverable as $1 + \mathrm{leftArm.length}$.

**Endpoint uniqueness without decidable equality.** The LN's "the walk contains both endnodes exactly once" most naturally reads as `support.count v = 1 ∧ support.count w = 1`, but `List.count` requires `[DecidableEq α]` and the vertex type `α : Type*` is arbitrary throughout chapter 3 (chapter 4+ uses real-valued nodes, which are not decidably equal). The decidability-free phrasing `v ∉ π.support.tail ∧ w ∉ π.support.dropLast` captures the same content — combined with the always-true placement of $v$ at position 0 and $w$ at position $n$ in `support`, it forces each endnode to appear exactly once.

**`bifurcationSource` is `Option α`-valued and `noncomputable`.** The source is `some bw.m'` (the LN's $v_k$) when the chosen witness's hinge is `backward` (LN's $\hut$), and `none` when it is `bidir` (LN's $\huh$, where the LN does not assign a source). The witness is selected from `Nonempty (BifurcationWitness π)` via `Classical.choice`, hence `noncomputable`. The LN does not claim uniqueness of the hinge, so the extractor is well-defined only up to classical choice. Every downstream use of the source either names a candidate via an outer existential (`∃ π, π.IsBifurcation ∧ π.bifurcationSource = some c`) or tests bifurcation existence without inspecting the source, so the choice ambiguity never surfaces.
